title: The Road to Sekigahara
bullets: Toyotomi Hideyoshi, peasant-born unifier of Japan, died in 1598 leaving a five-year-old heir and a five-regent council that immediately fractured into the Eastern and Western coalitions that fought at Sekigahara.

Hideyoshi's two failed Korean invasions of 1592 and 1597 mobilized roughly 300,000 Japanese troops, killed perhaps a million Koreans, drained the Toyotomi treasury, and produced no territorial gains whatsoever.

The Battle of Sekigahara on October 21, 1600 pitted roughly 80,000 Tokugawa Ieyasu loyalists against 80,000 Western Army troops under bureaucrat-general Ishida Mitsunari, and was decided by the prearranged treachery of Kobayakawa Hideaki around noon.

Tokugawa Ieyasu was appointed shogun in 1603, choosing as his administrative capital not Kyoto and not Osaka but Edo, then a small fishing village on a swampy bay that within a century would be the largest city in the world.

The Sieges of Osaka in 1614–15 destroyed the Toyotomi heir Hideyori, his mother Yodo-dono, and his seven-year-old illegitimate grandson, extinguishing the Toyotomi line and removing the last political alternative to the Tokugawa.

Ieyasu's 1615 Buke Shohatto regulated daimyo conduct and his Ikkoku Ichijō Rei required daimyo to demolish all but one castle per province, breaking the military infrastructure of the warring-states era.
Ieyasu died in 1616 and was enshrined as the deity Tōshō Daigongen at the Nikkō Tōshō-gū shrine north of Edo, becoming the cult center of the dynasty that would rule Japan for the next 252 years.

Sekigahara in 1600 was almost exactly contemporary with the founding of the English East India Company, the height of Shah Abbas the Great's reign in Safavid Persia, and the late phase of Akbar the Great's Mughal Empire.
subtitle: Hideyoshi dies, Sekigahara is fought, Ieyasu builds Edo and extinguishes the Toyotomi

title: The Tokugawa System
bullets: The bakuhan system divided Japan between the central shogunate (bakufu) in Edo and roughly 270 daimyo domains (han), with daimyo running their territories autonomously under tight Tokugawa supervision on a narrow set of matters.

Daimyo were sorted into three categories: shinpan Tokugawa branches, fudai hereditary vassals who monopolized shogunate offices, and tozama outside lords like Satsuma and Chōshū who held the largest domains but were excluded from central power for 265 years.

Iemitsu's 1635 sankin-kōtai system required every daimyo to spend alternate years in Edo and to leave their wives and heirs there permanently as hostages, consuming an estimated 50–75% of daimyo annual income.

Sankin-kōtai built the Gokaidō highway network — the Tōkaidō, Nakasendō, Kōshū, Ōshū, and Nikkō roads — and grew Edo to perhaps 1.2 million people by 1700, almost certainly the largest city in the world at that point.

The shi-nō-kō-shō four-class order ranked samurai, peasants, artisans, and merchants in descending Confucian rank — placing merchants at the bottom even as they accumulated most of Japan's wealth by the eighteenth century.
The eta and hinin pollution castes handled leather, butchering, and execution work; their descendants, now called burakumin, still face residual social discrimination in modern Japan.
The terauke temple registration system required every household to register at a Buddhist temple, creating a comprehensive population register two centuries before any European state attempted one.
The Tokugawa state founded the Shōheikō academy in Edo in 1690 to train officials in Zhu Xi Neo-Confucian orthodoxy, providing ideological cover for the entire hierarchical order.
subtitle: the bakuhan structure, the sankin-kotai system, and the four-class hierarchy

title: Sakoku and the Christian Purge
bullets: Christianity arrived in Japan in 1549 with the Spanish Jesuit Francis Xavier, and by the 1614 expulsion edict an estimated 300,000 to 500,000 Japanese Christians lived in the country, concentrated heavily in Kyushu.

Ieyasu's 1614 Christian Expulsion Edict triggered systematic atrocities including upside-down suspension over excrement pits, scalding by Unzen sulfur springs, and slow crucifixion — the Catholic Church recognizes 188 Japanese martyrs from this period with hundreds more under consideration.

The fumi-e picture-trampling test required suspected Christians to step on bronze plaques bearing images of Christ or the Virgin Mary; refusal meant arrest and execution, and the test continued at Nagasaki into the nineteenth century.
The Shimabara Rebellion of 1637–38, led by the seventeen-year-old Christian Amakusa Shirō, ended with the shogunate killing roughly 37,000 rebels and their families at Hara Castle in April 1638.

Iemitsu's 1639 sakoku edict expelled the Portuguese from Japan; when a Portuguese embassy arrived from Macau in 1640 to negotiate reopening trade, sixty-one of the seventy delegation members were beheaded on arrival.

After 1641, all European trade was confined to about twenty Dutch employees on the artificial fan-shaped island of Dejima in Nagasaki harbor, who were prohibited from practicing Christianity in any visible form including burials.
The four mouths of authorized contact were Nagasaki for the Dutch and Chinese, Tsushima for Korea, Satsuma for Ryukyu and indirect China trade, and Matsumae for the Ainu and indirect Russian contact.

Sakoku was not a uniquely Japanese eccentricity but a recognizable variant of broader East Asian closure policies — the Ming haijin and the Joseon Korean closure ran on similar principles but Japan enforced its system more systematically and longer.
subtitle: the Christian century ends, the Shimabara Rebellion is crushed, and the country closes

title: Genroku Culture
bullets: By 1700 Japan was probably the most urbanized country in the world, with roughly 10–12% of the population living in cities of 10,000 or more — a higher proportion than any European country except the Dutch Republic.

Ukiyo, the floating world, was a Buddhist pun: the older usage meant the sad world of suffering, repurposed via homophone to mean the floating world of pleasure, transient beauty, and aesthetic refinement.

The Yoshiwara pleasure quarter in Edo, relocated north of the city after the Great Meireki Fire of 1657 killed 100,000 people, housed several thousand licensed prostitutes and functioned as the cultural laboratory of the floating world.

Kabuki was banned for women in 1629 and for young male performers in 1652, and developed thereafter as an exclusively adult male art form with onnagata female-role specialists trained in highly stylized femininity.

Chikamatsu Monzaemon's The Love Suicides at Sonezaki, premiered 1703, dramatized a real Osaka double suicide from the previous month and triggered an actual epidemic of imitation suicides that the shogunate banned dramatizing.

Matsuo Bashō walked over 1,500 miles through northern Honshu in 1689 and produced Oku no Hosomichi, the single most influential work in Japanese literature, blending prose travel narrative with haiku written along the way.

Ihara Saikaku's 1682 Life of an Amorous Man and his subsequent ukiyo-zōshi novels essentially invented Japanese realist popular fiction, presenting the merchant world for the merchant world in tones simultaneously cynical and sympathetic.
The Dōjima Rice Exchange in Osaka, formally licensed by the shogunate in 1697, was the world's first organized commodity futures market, predating the Chicago Board of Trade by 151 years.

Tokugawa Tsunayoshi the Dog Shogun made killing a dog a capital crime and built the Nakano Dog Kennels housing 50,000 strays at public expense — his Edicts of Compassion for Living Beings made him a national joke.
subtitle: the floating world, kabuki, Bashō's haiku, and the world's first futures market

title: The 47 Rōnin
bullets: The Akō incident began on March 14, 1701 when daimyo Asano Naganori drew his short sword in a corridor of Edo Castle and slashed the ceremonial official Kira Yoshinaka across the forehead and shoulder.

Asano was ordered that same day to commit seppuku, his Akō domain confiscated and his retainers dispossessed, while Kira received no punishment at all — a violation of the kenka ryōseibai principle of equal punishment for both sides of a quarrel.

Ōishi Kuranosuke, the Akō chamberlain who chose to lead vendetta rather than accept dispossession, spent over a year in Kyoto pretending to drunken degeneracy in a brothel district to convince Kira's spies that no revenge was coming.

On the night of December 14, 1702, in a heavy snowfall, forty-six rōnin attacked Kira's Edo mansion, found him hiding in a charcoal storage shed in his nightclothes, and beheaded him with the same short sword Asano had used.

The shogunate's compromise verdict in February 1703 permitted the rōnin to commit seppuku rather than be executed as common criminals — they died on March 20, 1703 and were buried beside Asano at Sengakuji temple.
The story produced dozens of plays beginning within weeks of the verdict, with the most famous version Kanadehon Chūshingura written in 1748 and filmed in over fifty cinema adaptations since.

Yamamoto Tsunetomo's Hagakure, recorded between 1709 and 1716 in the same generation as the Akō incident, codified bushido at exactly the moment when actual samurai had become unemployed bureaucrats with no wars to fight.

The Akō incident's structural analogue is Cervantes's Don Quixote, published in 1605 — both about people whose social role has lost its function and who respond by performing the form even more intensely, but the Japanese tradition canonized what Cervantes treated as comedy.
subtitle: Asano draws his sword, Ōishi waits a year, and bushido finds its definitive story

title: Rangaku and the Knowledge Trickle
bullets: In 1771 the physicians Sugita Genpaku, Maeno Ryōtaku, and Nakagawa Jun'an attended an Edo autopsy with a borrowed Dutch anatomy textbook and discovered that the Western book matched the cadaver exactly while their Chinese medical training did not.
The three physicians spent three years translating the textbook as Kaitai Shinsho, published in 1774 — the first Western scientific work translated directly from a European language into Japanese.

Tokugawa Yoshimune's 1720 partial lifting of the Western book ban opened the channel through which the rangaku Dutch-learning tradition would absorb European science, medicine, and technology for the next 130 years.

The hereditary Nagasaki Dutch interpreters — about a hundred individuals managing all communication at Dejima — became the seed institution from which Dutch-language competence radiated to the few hundred rangaku scholars by 1820 and few thousand by 1850.
Inō Tadataka's coastal survey of Japan (1800–1816), conducted on foot by a fifty-five-year-old retired sake brewer, produced maps so accurate that the British Royal Navy still used them in the 1860s.

Hiraga Gennai built Japan's first electrostatic generator (the elekiteru) by reverse-engineering it from Dutch books, painted in oil, wrote satirical novels and kabuki plays, and ended his life in prison for killing a colleague in a fit of rage.
Philipp Franz von Siebold, the German physician at Dejima from 1823 to 1829, taught Western medicine to over fifty Japanese students before being expelled for acquiring Japanese maps.

The lag between European discovery and Japanese transmission was always 30 to 80 years through the rangaku channel, but the channel was consistent — meaning Japan was systematically receiving Western technical knowledge on delay rather than on a one-time basis when Perry arrived.
subtitle: Sugita's anatomy text, Inō's coastal survey, and the hidden infrastructure of modernization

title: The Long Decline
bullets: The bakufu's tax base was about 4 million koku of rice from a fixed land area while expenses grew over time, producing a chronic gap between fixed agricultural income and expanding monetary expenses that broke the system fiscally.

Currency debasement under Tsunayoshi in 1695 reduced gold and silver content by roughly a third, producing one-time fiscal windfalls that became inflation, eroded samurai stipend purchasing power, and required successive reform programs that mostly failed.

The Kyōhō Reforms of Yoshimune, the Kansei Reforms of Matsudaira Sadanobu, and the Tenpō Reforms of Mizuno Tadakuni each attempted variations of austerity, agricultural improvement, and Confucian moral retrenchment — each produced short-term fiscal improvement and was then partially reversed.
Peasant ikki uprisings rose from perhaps 30 to 50 documented uprisings per decade in the early Edo period to over 200 per decade in the 1830s, tracking the fiscal squeeze on the rural majority.

The Tenmei famine of 1782–1788 killed approximately 920,000 people across northeastern Japan, triggered by cold summers from the 1783 Iceland Laki eruption and the August 1783 Mount Asama eruption that killed 1,400 people directly.

The Tenpō famine of 1833–1837 triggered Ōshio Heihachirō's one-day armed uprising in Osaka in February 1837 — the first armed revolt by a former bakufu official in two centuries, exposing dissent inside the shogunate's own administrative class.
The 1808 Phaeton Incident, when a British naval frigate sailed into Nagasaki harbor demanding supplies, drove the local magistrate to suicide and shocked the bakufu into building coastal defenses.

The 1825 Edict to Repel Foreign Ships triggered the 1837 Morrison Incident, when an unarmed American ship returning Japanese castaways was fired upon — the public backlash led to the Bansha no Goku purge of rangaku scholars who had criticized the policy.
subtitle: fiscal crisis, peasant uprisings, the Tenmei famine, and the foreign-ship problem

title: The Black Ships and the End
bullets: On July 8, 1853, US Commodore Matthew Perry sailed four ships including two side-wheel steam frigates into Edo Bay and demanded Japan open to trade — ending 214 years of sakoku at the muzzle of an American gun.

Chief minister Abe Masahiro circulated Perry's letters to all the daimyo asking advice, the first time the bakufu had publicly admitted it could not handle a problem alone — a constitutional revolution that invited the daimyo into foreign-policy decisions.

The Treaty of Kanagawa signed March 31, 1854 ended sakoku as a legal regime, opening the ports of Shimoda and Hakodate to American consuls and supplies, with a most-favored-nation clause that automatically extended any future privileges to other powers.

The Harris Treaty of July 29, 1858 granted American extraterritoriality, fixed Japanese tariffs at roughly 5%, and opened five ports — within weeks Britain, France, the Netherlands, and Russia signed near-identical Ansei Treaties forming the unequal treaty regime.

Chief minister Ii Naosuke was assassinated outside the Sakurada Gate of Edo Castle on a snowy morning in March 1860 by Mito loyalists avenging the Ansei Purge — proving that the bakufu could no longer protect even its highest officials.

The Tosa rōnin Sakamoto Ryōma brokered the secret Satsuma-Chōshū Alliance in March 1866, bringing the two great southwestern domains into the coalition that would overthrow the Tokugawa within two years.

The fifteenth shogun Tokugawa Yoshinobu formally returned political authority to the emperor in November 1867 in the Taisei Hōkan, but the Boshin War of January 1868 to June 1869 was still required to crush bakufu loyalists.

Sixteen-year-old Emperor Meiji's Charter Oath of April 1868 promised public assembly, abolition of class restrictions, and the seeking of knowledge throughout the world — within forty years Japan defeated China, defeated Russia, and annexed Korea.

Fukuzawa Yukichi, raised entirely under the Tokugawa system, crossed the Pacific in 1860 on the Japanese-built Kanrin Maru, founded Keio University, and ended up on the 10,000-yen note — one biography summarizing what the Edo period was for.
subtitle: Perry arrives, the shogunate cracks, and the Meiji Restoration ends 265 years of Tokugawa rule
